name: Zonules.com Route Registry
note: No public page may exist without an entry here. Draft pages must not appear in navigation, sitemap, or hreflang output.

routes.meta_description: Zonules.com is a developed category asset: a governed reference system and Category Intelligence Factory for focus, perception, and visual truth. Strategic inquiries only.

routes.seo_title: Zonules.com — The Hidden System That Makes Vision Possible
routes.meta_description: Zonules are not vision. They are what makes vision possible. A sovereign visual reference system mapping the hidden structures that hold focus across anatomy, perception, and machine vision.
Run the Focus Integrity Assessment: the same five criteria decide whether focus holds in the eye, in perception, or in a machine. Deterministic, computed locally, no data collected.
The zonules of Zinn suspend the crystalline lens in precise tension, making accommodation and optical focus possible.
The ciliary body anchors the zonules and houses the ciliary muscle that governs their tension.
The crystalline lens refracts light onto the retina.
Accommodation is how the eye changes optical power.
The lens capsule is the elastic envelope into which the zonular fibers insert.
Visual perception turns visual input into stable meaning.
Machine vision lets artificial systems interpret images.
Selective attention holds a focal target steady against competing stimuli.
Figure-ground segregates a scene into object and background.
Deepfake detection asks whether what a system sees is real.
Image provenance records where an image came from.
How focus fails across anatomy, perception, and machine vision.
The complete governed index of Zonules.com reference units.
How Zonules.com governs its reference corpus: the route, claim, and source registries; the focus-integrity ontology and standard; and the blocking quality gate that enforces them.
How Zonules.com classifies sources and governs claims.
The editorial rules that govern which content is added to Zonules.com.
Zonules.com is an educational reference, not medical advice.
The cornea provides two-thirds of the eye's refractive power.
The retina converts focused light into neural signals.
Rods and cones that transduce light into neural signals.
The optic nerve carries retinal axons toward the brain.
Perceptual constancy maintains stable object representations despite changing sensory conditions.
How the visual system groups elements into wholes.
Computer vision enables machines to extract meaning from images.
Image classification assigns categorical labels to images.
The iris controls the amount of light entering the eye.
Maintains the focal distance between lens and retina.
The macula is the central high-acuity zone of the retina.
Depth perception constructs a 3D interpretation from 2D retinal signals.
Failing to notice a fully visible unexpected stimulus.
Visual working memory stores and maintains visual information for active use.
Object detection identifies and localizes instances in an image.
Imperceptible perturbations that cause machine vision to fail.
The aqueous humor nourishes the cornea and lens and maintains intraocular pressure.
The primary visual cortex performs the first cortical stage of seeing.
The pupillary light reflex automatically constricts the pupil in response to light.
Sustained attention is the capacity to maintain focus over time.
Failure to detect a large scene change across a disruption.
Visual saliency is the automatic prioritization of scene elements by feature contrast.
Calibration holds a machine vision system's spatial reference frame in place.
Domain shift is when deployment inputs differ from training inputs, degrading performance.
The fovea centralis is the small depression at the center of the macula where cone density is highest and visual acuity is greatest. The anatomical zero-point of focused sight.
The optic chiasm is the X-shaped junction where nasal retinal fibres from each eye cross to the opposite hemisphere, routing the visual signal for binocular integration.
The optic tract carries post-chiasmatic visual signals from the optic chiasm to the lateral geniculate nucleus.
The lateral geniculate nucleus is the thalamic relay station where visual signals are processed and forwarded to the primary visual cortex.
Binocular vision is the integration of input from two eyes into a single, spatially coherent visual field, enabling stereoscopic depth perception.
The oculomotor system controls eye movements to direct gaze, placing the fovea on targets of interest.
The sclera is the tough outer coat of the eye that maintains its spherical shape, protecting the optical contents and providing attachment for the extraocular muscles.
The choroid is the highly vascular layer between the sclera and retina, supplying oxygen and nutrients to the outer retinal layers.

Pattern recognition matches sensory input against stored templates and is a primary site of interpretation failure. A governed reference unit on perceptual clarity in the Zonules focus-integrity framework.

Visual inference fills gaps using prior probability, risking the override of signal fidelity by expectation. A governed reference unit on perceptual interpretation in the Zonules focus-integrity framework.

Perceptual load sets the effective aperture of attention, determining which stimuli are processed in detail. A governed reference unit on attentional accommodation in the Zonules focus-integrity framework.

Top-down processing uses prior knowledge to guide interpretation and can systematically distort an accurate signal. A governed reference unit on perceptual interpretation in the Zonules focus-integrity framework.

Attention capture is the involuntary, salience-driven reorientation of attention by competing visual noise. A governed reference unit on perceptual signal-noise separation in the Zonules focus-integrity framework.

Perceptual error is the observable output of interpretation failure, where a clear signal is assigned an incorrect meaning. A governed reference unit on perceptual clarity in the Zonules focus-integrity framework.
When the visual signal supports multiple readings.
Visual scene parsing segments, groups, and assigns spatial relations to construct scene meaning. A governed reference unit on perceptual interpretation in the Zonules focus-integrity framework.

routes.seo_title: Optical Flow — Tracking Motion Across the Visual Field | Zonules.com
routes.meta_description: Optical flow is the pattern of apparent motion of image brightness computed from frame-to-frame differences. Classified FIO-01 Suspension.

routes.seo_title: Scene Understanding — Structured Semantic Interpretation in Machine Vision | Zonules.com
routes.meta_description: Scene understanding is the capacity of a vision system to produce a structured, semantically labelled representation of an image. Classified FIO-05.

routes.seo_title: Synthetic Media — Machine-Generated Images and the Provenance Problem | Zonules.com
routes.meta_description: Synthetic media is visual content generated by machine learning systems. Its indistinguishability from authentic capture is a provenance integrity failure. Classified FIO-04.

routes.seo_title: Visual Grounding — Linking Language to the Visual World | Zonules.com
routes.meta_description: Visual grounding is the task of linking linguistic expressions to their corresponding image regions. Classified FIO-05 Interpretation.

routes.seo_title: Semantic Segmentation — Dense Pixel-Level Scene Labelling | Zonules.com
routes.meta_description: Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel in an image. The machine-vision instantiation of visual scene parsing at pixel level. Classified FIO-05.

routes.seo_title: Ophthalmic AI — Machine Vision Applied to the Eye | Zonules.com
routes.meta_description: Ophthalmic AI applies machine vision and deep learning to ocular imaging data for diagnostic and screening purposes. Classified FIO-05.

routes.seo_title: Medical Imaging AI — High-Stakes Machine Interpretation of Clinical Images | Zonules.com
routes.meta_description: Medical imaging AI applies machine vision to clinical images for diagnostic support. Carries the highest-stakes interpretation risks in the FIO framework. Classified FIO-05.

routes.seo_title: Neural Network Interpretability — Understanding What Machine Vision Actually Sees | Zonules.com
routes.meta_description: Neural network interpretability is the field concerned with understanding the internal representations of deep learning models applied to visual tasks. Classified FIO-05.
Retinal ganglion cells are the output neurons of the retina whose axons form the optic nerve and carry the encoded visual signal to the brain.
The blind spot is the region of the retina where the optic nerve exits and which contains no photoreceptors, producing a gap in the visual field.
Saccades are rapid ballistic eye movements that shift the high-acuity fovea from one point of interest to another to maintain focus on changing targets.
Smooth pursuit is a voluntary eye movement that tracks a moving target to keep its image stable on the fovea, matching eye velocity to target velocity.
The extraocular muscles are the six muscles that rotate each eye within the orbit to direct gaze and maintain binocular alignment.
The optic radiations are the white-matter fiber tracts carrying visual signals from the lateral geniculate nucleus to the primary visual cortex.
The retinal pigment epithelium is a single layer of pigmented cells between the photoreceptors and the choroid that supports photoreceptor function.
The conjunctiva is the thin transparent mucous membrane covering the sclera and lining the inner eyelids, protecting and lubricating the ocular surface.
The trabecular meshwork is the spongy tissue at the iridocorneal angle through which aqueous humor drains, regulating intraocular pressure.
Ocular fixation is the maintenance of gaze on a single location, holding the target's image on the fovea through tiny stabilizing eye movements.
The tear film is the thin fluid layer coating the ocular surface that maintains a smooth optical surface on the cornea and protects it.
Contrast sensitivity is the visual system's ability to distinguish luminance differences between adjacent regions, varying with spatial frequency.
Visual acuity is the capacity to resolve fine spatial detail, highest at the fovea and dependent on optics, photoreceptor sampling, and neural processing.
Motion perception is the process by which the visual system detects and interprets movement in the visual field through direction- and speed-selective mechanisms.
Color perception is the visual system's construction of color from the spectral composition of light, shaped by opponent processing and context.
Visual masking is the reduction or elimination of one stimulus's visibility by another presented in close spatial or temporal proximity.
Visual search is the task of locating a target among distractors, ranging from efficient parallel search to effortful serial search.
Apparent motion is the perception of movement produced by stationary stimuli presented in rapid succession, the basis of motion in film and displays.
The binding problem concerns how the visual system combines separately processed features such as color, shape, and motion into unified object representations.
Bistable perception occurs when a single unchanging stimulus alternates between two stable interpretations, as in reversible figures.
Perceptual priming is the facilitation of recognizing a stimulus due to prior exposure to a related stimulus, often without conscious awareness.
Visual illusions are systematic discrepancies between the perceived and physical properties of a stimulus, revealing the visual system's constructive assumptions.
Vision-language models are machine learning systems that jointly process images and text to perform tasks such as captioning and visual question answering.
Pose estimation is the machine vision task of inferring the spatial configuration of objects or human bodies, such as joint keypoints, from images.
Depth estimation is the machine vision task of inferring distance to scene points from images, using stereo, motion, or learned monocular cues.
Visual SLAM is the technique by which a system builds a map of an environment while tracking its own position using camera input.
Feature extraction transforms raw image data into representations that capture informative structure for downstream machine vision tasks.
Transfer learning is the reuse of a model trained on one task or dataset to improve learning on a related task, commonly by adapting pretrained features.
Out-of-distribution detection is the task of identifying inputs that differ from a model's training distribution, important for safe deployment.
Visual tracking is the machine vision task of following the position of one or more targets across video frames despite appearance change and occlusion.
Data augmentation expands training data by applying label-preserving transformations to existing images, improving robustness to input variation.
Image registration is the process of aligning two or more images into a common spatial coordinate frame, critical in fields such as medical imaging.
Scene graph generation is the machine vision task of producing a structured graph of objects and their relationships in an image to support reasoning.
A receptive field is the region of sensory space within which a stimulus activates a given sensory neuron.
Dark adaptation is the process by which the visual system increases its sensitivity after exposure to bright conditions, driven by photoreceptor recovery.
Light adaptation is the process by which the visual system reduces its sensitivity and adjusts its operating range in response to increased illumination.
Stereopsis is the perception of depth produced by the visual system's comparison of the slightly different images received by each eye.
Color constancy is the ability of the visual system to perceive the surface color of objects as stable despite changes in the spectral composition of the i
The visual field is the spatial extent of the environment that can be perceived when fixation is held steady, bounded by the anatomy of the retina and opti
Vergence refers to the disjunctive movements of the two eyes that maintain alignment of the visual axes on objects at different distances.
Predictive coding is a framework in which the brain continuously generates predictions about sensory input and propagates only the residual error signals n
Face perception is the visual processing of facial information, including identity, emotional expression, gaze direction, and age, supported by dedicated n
Object recognition is the visual process by which the identity of an object is determined from its projected two-dimensional image.
Scene recognition is the process by which the visual system assigns a global meaning to a visual environment from its spatial layout and statistical regula
Visual crowding is the impaired recognition of peripheral targets caused by the presence of nearby flanking elements.
In machine learning, an attention mechanism is a learned weighting function that selects the most relevant parts of an input representation before producin
The transformer is a neural network architecture that processes input sequences by stacking self-attention layers with feed-forward networks, replacing rec
A convolutional neural network is a neural network that uses learned, spatially-shared filters to detect local patterns at multiple scales.
Few-shot learning is the ability of a model to recognize new categories or tasks from a small number of examples after pre-training on a large corpus.
A generative model is a statistical model that learns the joint distribution of inputs and can produce new samples indistinguishable from training data.
Photopic vision is the visual function of the eye under well-lit conditions, mediated by cone photoreceptors.
Scotopic vision is the visual function of the eye under dim light conditions, mediated by rod photoreceptors.
Nystagmus is an involuntary, rhythmic oscillation of the eyes that impairs fixation stability and is the most common oculomotor disorder.
A scotoma is a region of absent or diminished vision within the visual field surrounded by areas of normal or relatively normal vision.
The accommodation reflex is the coordinated response of the visual system to bring a near object into focus, comprising lens accommodation, pupillary const
Active inference is the framework in which organisms minimize surprise by both updating internal models (perception) and acting on the environment to match
Mental imagery is the experience of visual representations in the absence of corresponding external stimulation.
Multisensory integration is the process by which the brain combines signals from different sensory modalities into a unified percept.
Spatial cognition is the set of mental processes by which the brain represents, navigates, and reasons about spatial relationships and locations in the env
Perceptual grouping is the visual system's tendency to organize elements of the visual scene into coherent units based on proximity, similarity, continuity
Temporal integration is the process by which the visual system combines stimuli presented within a brief time window into a single perceptual event.
Metacognition is the capacity to monitor and evaluate one's own cognitive processes, including assessing the confidence and accuracy of one's own perceptua
Self-supervised learning is a training paradigm in which a model learns representations by predicting parts of the input from other parts, without human-la
Contrastive learning is a self-supervised training objective that pulls representations of similar inputs together and pushes representations of dissimilar
Zero-shot learning is the ability of a model to correctly classify or describe inputs from categories it has never seen during training, using semantic des
Video understanding encompasses the machine vision tasks of recognizing actions, tracking objects, and modeling temporal relationships across video frame s
Action recognition is the task of identifying the action being performed in a video or sequence of images based on the temporal pattern of motion and appea
The optokinetic reflex is a reflexive eye movement that stabilizes the visual image during sustained whole-field motion by rotating the eyes in the directi
The vestibulo-ocular reflex stabilizes gaze during head movement by producing compensatory eye movements in the opposite direction to head rotation.
Spatial frequency describes the periodicity of luminance variation across space, measured in cycles per degree of visual angle, and is a fundamental descri
The contrast threshold is the minimum difference in luminance between a target and its background that is reliably detectable by the visual system.
Visual resolution is the ability of the eye to distinguish fine detail, measured as the minimum angular separation of two points perceived as distinct.
Bottom-up processing refers to perceptual analysis driven by the properties of the sensory input itself, proceeding from local features to global interpret
Cognitive load is the total amount of mental effort being used in working memory at a given time, limiting the capacity available for perception and task p
The attentional blink is the temporary impairment in detecting a second target that occurs when it follows a first target within roughly 200-500 ms in a ra
Perceptual adaptation is the process by which the visual system recalibrates its sensitivity or interpretation framework following prolonged exposure to a
Visual attention is the selective processing of part of the visual scene at the expense of other parts, determining what receives deeper analysis and reach
Spatial attention is the selective enhancement of processing for stimuli at a particular location in the visual field, operating like a spotlight that brig
Feature-based attention is the selective enhancement of processing for a particular visual feature value — a color, orientation, or motion direction — acro
Image quality assessment is the task of measuring or predicting the perceptual quality of an image, including degradations from noise, blur, compression, a
Visual question answering is the task of producing a correct textual answer to a natural language question about the content of an image.
Instance segmentation is the task of simultaneously detecting, classifying, and producing pixel-level masks for each individual instance of each object cla
Gaze estimation is the task of determining the direction in which a person is looking from an image or video of their face or eyes.
Image synthesis is the computational generation of new images from noise, text descriptions, or partial inputs using learned statistical models.
